revision.text:
{{draft}}
'''Kashiko-dokoro''' (賢所, also read as Kensho{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is the location within the '''Imperial Palace''' (宮中) of Japan where the '''{{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡}}''', one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan|'''Three Sacred Treasures''']] (三種の神器) and the sacred mirror of the goddess '''[[Amaterasu]]''', is enshrined. Currently, it is one of the '''Kyūchū Sanden''' (宮中三殿) in the '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Before the modern era, it was also called '''Naishidokoro''' (内侍所){{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. '''Kashiko-dokoro''' (かしこどころ、けんしょ{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) is a place in the [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Emperor]]'s [[imperial court]] where the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡}}), one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan]] and the spirit of [[Amaterasu Omikami]], is enshrined. In the current [[Imperial Palace]] it is one of the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries}} {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. [[File:Three Palace Sanctuaries.jpg|290px]] == Overview == {{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}} == Overview == The {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries}} '''(Kyūchū Sanden)''' face south and consist of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' at the center, the '''Kōreiden''' to the west, and the '''Shinden''' to the east{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Among these three sanctuaries, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is regarded as the most sacred, and its floor is raised approximately one shaku (about 30 centimeters) higher than the others{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. The structure is divided into three sections: * '''Outer Chamber''' (外陣): Accessible only to members of the Imperial family. * '''Inner Chamber''' (内陣): Accessible only to the Emperor and Empress. * '''Innermost Chamber''' (内々陣): The space where the sacred mirror and '''Amaterasu''' are enshrined. The '''Inner Chamber''' of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' features two '''eternal lamps''' (常夜灯){{Sfn|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was also known as '''Naishidokoro''', referencing the sacred mirror's earlier placement in the '''Onmeiden''' before its relocation to a separate hall{{Efn2|The transition of the sacred mirror from the Emperor’s chamber to the Onmeiden, and later its designation as Naishidokoro, is detailed in several sources, such as the {{ill|Sarashina Nikki|ja|更級日記}} and {{ill|The Tale of Genji|ja|源氏物語}}'s "Makibashira" chapter{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}.}}. Various kanji have been used for "Kashiko-dokoro," including '''恐所''', '''畏所''', '''威所''', '''尊所''', and '''貴所'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Daily offerings ('''Nikkū''') are conducted in the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' by the '''Naishoten''', and Imperial rituals ('''Myōchō Goyōhai''') are performed daily without interruption{{Sfn|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Additionally, special ceremonies such as the '''Seasonal Festivals''' (旬祭), the '''Kashiko-dokoro Ritual of Kanname-sai''' in October, and the '''Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura Ritual''' in December are held annually{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == Overview == The Three Palace Sanctuaries face south, with Kashiko-dokoro in the center, Kōreiden to the west, and Shinden to the east {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Kashiko-dokoro is considered the most noble building among the three sanctuaries, and its floor is designed to be about one shaku (approximately 30.3 cm) higher than the other two sanctuaries {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. Each sanctuary is divided into the '''outer sanctuary''' (a room where only [[imperial family]] members are allowed to enter), the '''inner sanctuary''' (a room where only the Emperor and Empress are allowed to enter), and the '''innermost sanctuary''' (a room where [[Amaterasu Omikami]] is enshrined). In the inner sanctuary of Kashiko-dokoro, there are two {{ill|eternal lamps|ja|常夜灯}} {{Sfnp|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, it was also called Naishidokoro ({{ill|内侍所|ja|内侍所}}) {{Efn2|神鏡は天皇の御座所に祀られていたが、その後に別殿の[[温明殿]]に移され、内侍所とも称されるようになった{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}。例えば『{{ill|更級日記|ja|更級日記}}』に内侍所とある{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=22}}。『{{ill|源氏物語|ja|源氏物語}}』の「{{ill|真木柱|ja|真木柱}}」にも内侍所とあり、11月には祭祀が夥しく行われていたことが反映されている{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}。なお、別殿に移された時期について、『{{ill|江家次第|ja|江家次第}}』や『{{ill|禁秘抄|ja|禁秘抄}}』は垂仁天皇の代とし、『{{ill|撰集抄|ja|撰集抄}}』は{{ill|宇多天皇|ja|宇多天皇}}の代としている{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|pp=59-60}}。}}. Various characters were used for "Kashiko-dokoro" such as "恐所," "畏所," "威所," "尊所," and "貴所" {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Every morning, rituals are conducted by the {{ill|naishoten|ja|内掌典}} and [[Shinto ritual|shinji]] are performed by an envoy dispatched by the Emperor without fail {{Sfnp|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Various rituals are also conducted, such as the Emperor's worship on the first day of the month, the [[Kanname-sai|Kanname-sai Kashiko-dokoro ceremony]] (a grand festival) in October, and the [[Kashiko-dokoro Misogi|Kashiko-dokoro Misogi ceremony]] (a minor festival) in December {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == History == The sacred mirror bestowed by [[Amaterasu Omikami]] has been enshrined within the Imperial Palace by successive generations of emperors according to the "Same Bed, Same Hall" divine decree {{Sfnp|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], during the reign of the tenth Emperor [[Suinin|Sujin]], the sacred mirror was moved outside the palace for enshrinement due to "living together being uneasy" {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|この神鏡は{{ill|垂仁天皇|ja|垂仁天皇}}の代に{{ill|伊勢国|ja|伊勢国}}の{{ill|五十鈴川|ja|五十鈴川}}上流に移され、後に{{ill|伊勢神宮|ja|伊勢神宮}}となる{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}。}}. According to the {{ill|Kogo Shuui|ja|古語拾遺}}, at that time, a duplicate of the sacred mirror was newly cast for enshrinement in the palace {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=54}}. From the mid-Heian period onward, with the burning of the {{ill|Dairi|ja|内裏}} and other reasons, temporary palaces were established, and with them, Kashiko-dokoro was also relocated to appropriate buildings {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. In later years, Kashiko-dokoro was moved to the {{ill|Shunkoden|ja|春興殿}}, and according to the descriptions in the {{ill|Meimosho|ja|名目抄}} by {{ill|Toin Sanehiro|ja|洞院実熙}}, by the early Muromachi period, Shunkoden had become Kashiko-dokoro {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. From the Jōkyō and Genroku periods of the Edo era, commoners were allowed to visit Naishidokoro on [[Setsubun]], and it became bustling with many visitors {{Sfnp|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In the early Meiji period, it was relocated to Tokyo, and according to a decree in September of the 4th year of Meiji (1871), shrines including Kashiko-dokoro were constructed {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. == History == The sacred mirror, bestowed by '''Amaterasu''', was enshrined in the Imperial Palace per the divine command ('''Same Chamber, Same Hall''') and has been worshiped by successive emperors{{Sfn|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the '''Nihon Shoki''', during the reign of Emperor Sujin (崇神天皇), the sacred mirror was relocated outside the palace for more secure worship{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|This mirror was eventually moved to the '''Ise Shrine''' during the reign of Emperor Suinin (垂仁天皇){{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}.}}. The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' experienced relocations due to the destruction of the '''Dairi''' (Imperial Residence) in the Heian period and the establishment of '''temporary residences''' ('''Satodairi'''){{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. By the early Muromachi period, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' had moved to the '''Shunki-den''', as noted in '''Naimokushō''' by '''Dōin Sanehiro'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. During the '''Ōnin War''', the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was temporarily relocated to the '''Muromachi Palace''', where it regained its position as the priority site for Imperial rituals{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52-53}}. By the Edo period, public access to the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was permitted during the '''Setsubun''' festival, attracting numerous visitors{{Sfn|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In 1871, during the Meiji era, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was officially reconstructed as part of the newly relocated '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. ==See also== * {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro Riding Car|ja|賢所乗御車}} * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} * {{ill|Kyūchū Sanden|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries}} * {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura|ja|賢所御神楽}} * {{ill|Kinri Koban|ja|禁裏小番}} == Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}} ===References=== {{Reflist|3}} == References == ; Books * {{Cite revision.text:
{{draft}}
'''Kashiko-dokoro''' (賢所, also read as Kensho{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is the location within the '''Imperial Palace''' (宮中) of Japan where the '''{{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡|12=draft}}''', one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan|'''Three Sacred Treasures''']] (三種の神器) and the sacred mirror of the goddess '''[[Amaterasu]]''', is enshrined. Currently, it is one of the '''Kyūchū Sanden''' (宮中三殿) in the '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Before the modern era, it was also called '''Naishidokoro''' (内侍所){{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. '''Kashiko-dokoro''' (かしこどころ、けんしょ{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) is a place in the [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Emperor]]'s [[imperial court]] where the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡|12=draft}}), one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan]] and the spirit of [[Amaterasu Omikami]], is enshrined. In the current [[Imperial Palace]] it is one of the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. [[File:Three Palace Sanctuaries.jpg|290px]] == Overview == {{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}} == Overview == The {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft}} '''(Kyūchū Sanden)''' face south and consist of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' at the center, the '''Kōreiden''' to the west, and the '''Shinden''' to the east{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Among these three sanctuaries, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is regarded as the most sacred, and its floor is raised approximately one shaku (about 30 centimeters) higher than the others{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. The structure is divided into three sections: * '''Outer Chamber''' (外陣): Accessible only to members of the Imperial family. * '''Inner Chamber''' (内陣): Accessible only to the Emperor and Empress. * '''Innermost Chamber''' (内々陣): The space where the sacred mirror and '''Amaterasu''' are enshrined. The '''Inner Chamber''' of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' features two '''eternal lamps''' (常夜灯){{Sfn|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was also known as '''Naishidokoro''', referencing the sacred mirror's earlier placement in the '''Onmeiden''' before its relocation to a separate hall{{Efn2|The transition of the sacred mirror from the Emperor’s chamber to the Onmeiden, and later its designation as Naishidokoro, is detailed in several sources, such as the {{ill|Sarashina Nikki|ja|更級日記|12=draft}} and {{ill|The Tale of Genji|ja|源氏物語|12=draft}}'s "Makibashira" chapter{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}.}}. Various kanji have been used for "Kashiko-dokoro," including '''恐所''', '''畏所''', '''威所''', '''尊所''', and '''貴所'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Daily offerings ('''Nikkū''') are conducted in the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' by the '''Naishoten''', and Imperial rituals ('''Myōchō Goyōhai''') are performed daily without interruption{{Sfn|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Additionally, special ceremonies such as the '''Seasonal Festivals''' (旬祭), the '''Kashiko-dokoro Ritual of Kanname-sai''' in October, and the '''Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura Ritual''' in December are held annually{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == Overview == The Three Palace Sanctuaries face south, with Kashiko-dokoro in the center, Kōreiden to the west, and Shinden to the east {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Kashiko-dokoro is considered the most noble building among the three sanctuaries, and its floor is designed to be about one shaku (approximately 30.3 cm) higher than the other two sanctuaries {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. Each sanctuary is divided into the '''outer sanctuary''' (a room where only [[imperial family]] members are allowed to enter), the '''inner sanctuary''' (a room where only the Emperor and Empress are allowed to enter), and the '''innermost sanctuary''' (a room where [[Amaterasu Omikami]] is enshrined). In the inner sanctuary of Kashiko-dokoro, there are two {{ill|eternal lamps|ja|常夜灯|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, it was also called Naishidokoro ({{ill|内侍所|ja|内侍所|12=draft}}) {{Efn2|神鏡は天皇の御座所に祀られていたが、その後に別殿の[[温明殿]]に移され、内侍所とも称されるようになった{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}。例えば『{{ill|更級日記|ja|更級日記|12=draft}}』に内侍所とある{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=22}}。『{{ill|源氏物語|ja|源氏物語|12=draft}}』の「{{ill|真木柱|ja|真木柱|12=draft}}」にも内侍所とあり、11月には祭祀が夥しく行われていたことが反映されている{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}。なお、別殿に移された時期について、『{{ill|江家次第|ja|江家次第|12=draft}}』や『{{ill|禁秘抄|ja|禁秘抄|12=draft}}』は垂仁天皇の代とし、『{{ill|撰集抄|ja|撰集抄|12=draft}}』は{{ill|宇多天皇|ja|宇多天皇|12=draft}}の代としている{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|pp=59-60}}。}}. Various characters were used for "Kashiko-dokoro" such as "恐所," "畏所," "威所," "尊所," and "貴所" {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Every morning, rituals are conducted by the {{ill|naishoten|ja|内掌典|12=draft}} and [[Shinto ritual|shinji]] are performed by an envoy dispatched by the Emperor without fail {{Sfnp|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Various rituals are also conducted, such as the Emperor's worship on the first day of the month, the [[Kanname-sai|Kanname-sai Kashiko-dokoro ceremony]] (a grand festival) in October, and the [[Kashiko-dokoro Misogi|Kashiko-dokoro Misogi ceremony]] (a minor festival) in December {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == History == The sacred mirror bestowed by [[Amaterasu Omikami]] has been enshrined within the Imperial Palace by successive generations of emperors according to the "Same Bed, Same Hall" divine decree {{Sfnp|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], during the reign of the tenth Emperor [[Suinin|Sujin]], the sacred mirror was moved outside the palace for enshrinement due to "living together being uneasy" {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|この神鏡は{{ill|垂仁天皇|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft}}の代に{{ill|伊勢国|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}の{{ill|五十鈴川|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft}}上流に移され、後に{{ill|伊勢神宮|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}となる{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}。}}. According to the {{ill|Kogo Shuui|ja|古語拾遺|12=draft}}, at that time, a duplicate of the sacred mirror was newly cast for enshrinement in the palace {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=54}}. From the mid-Heian period onward, with the burning of the {{ill|Dairi|ja|内裏|12=draft}} and other reasons, temporary palaces were established, and with them, Kashiko-dokoro was also relocated to appropriate buildings {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. In later years, Kashiko-dokoro was moved to the {{ill|Shunkoden|ja|春興殿|12=draft}}, and according to the descriptions in the {{ill|Meimosho|ja|名目抄|12=draft}} by {{ill|Toin Sanehiro|ja|洞院実熙|12=draft}}, by the early Muromachi period, Shunkoden had become Kashiko-dokoro {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. From the Jōkyō and Genroku periods of the Edo era, commoners were allowed to visit Naishidokoro on [[Setsubun]], and it became bustling with many visitors {{Sfnp|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In the early Meiji period, it was relocated to Tokyo, and according to a decree in September of the 4th year of Meiji (1871), shrines including Kashiko-dokoro were constructed {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. == History == The sacred mirror, bestowed by '''Amaterasu''', was enshrined in the Imperial Palace per the divine command ('''Same Chamber, Same Hall''') and has been worshiped by successive emperors{{Sfn|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the '''Nihon Shoki''', during the reign of Emperor Sujin (崇神天皇), the sacred mirror was relocated outside the palace for more secure worship{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|This mirror was eventually moved to the '''Ise Shrine''' during the reign of Emperor Suinin (垂仁天皇){{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}.}}. The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' experienced relocations due to the destruction of the '''Dairi''' (Imperial Residence) in the Heian period and the establishment of '''temporary residences''' ('''Satodairi'''){{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. By the early Muromachi period, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' had moved to the '''Shunki-den''', as noted in '''Naimokushō''' by '''Dōin Sanehiro'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. During the '''Ōnin War''', the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was temporarily relocated to the '''Muromachi Palace''', where it regained its position as the priority site for Imperial rituals{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52-53}}. By the Edo period, public access to the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was permitted during the '''Setsubun''' festival, attracting numerous visitors{{Sfn|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In 1871, during the Meiji era, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was officially reconstructed as part of the newly relocated '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. ==See also== * revision.text:
{{draft}}
'''Kashiko-dokoro''' (賢所, also read as Kensho{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is the location within the '''Imperial Palace''' (宮中) of Japan where the '''{{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡|12=draft}}''', one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan|'''Three Sacred Treasures''']] (三種の神器) and the sacred mirror of the goddess '''[[Amaterasu]]''', is enshrined. Currently, it is one of the '''Kyūchū Sanden''' (宮中三殿) in the '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Before the modern era, it was also called '''Naishidokoro''' (内侍所){{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. '''Kashiko-dokoro''' (かしこどころ、けんしょ{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) is a place in the [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Emperor]]'s [[imperial court]] where the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡|12=draft}}), one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan]] and the spirit of [[Amaterasu Omikami]], is enshrined. In the current [[Imperial Palace]] it is one of the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. [[File:Three Palace Sanctuaries.jpg|290px]] == Overview == {{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}} == Overview == The {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft}} '''(Kyūchū Sanden)''' face south and consist of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' at the center, the '''Kōreiden''' to the west, and the '''Shinden''' to the east{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Among these three sanctuaries, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is regarded as the most sacred, and its floor is raised approximately one shaku (about 30 centimeters) higher than the others{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. The structure is divided into three sections: * '''Outer Chamber''' (外陣): Accessible only to members of the Imperial family. * '''Inner Chamber''' (内陣): Accessible only to the Emperor and Empress. * '''Innermost Chamber''' (内々陣): The space where the sacred mirror and '''Amaterasu''' are enshrined. The '''Inner Chamber''' of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' features two '''eternal lamps''' (常夜灯){{Sfn|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was also known as '''Naishidokoro''', referencing the sacred mirror's earlier placement in the '''Onmeiden''' before its relocation to a separate hall{{Efn2|The transition of the sacred mirror from the Emperor’s chamber to the Onmeiden, and later its designation as Naishidokoro, is detailed in several sources, such as the {{ill|Sarashina Nikki|ja|更級日記|12=draft}} and {{ill|The Tale of Genji|ja|源氏物語|12=draft}}'s "Makibashira" chapter{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}.}}. Various kanji have been used for "Kashiko-dokoro," including '''恐所''', '''畏所''', '''威所''', '''尊所''', and '''貴所'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Daily offerings ('''Nikkū''') are conducted in the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' by the '''Naishoten''', and Imperial rituals ('''Myōchō Goyōhai''') are performed daily without interruption{{Sfn|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Additionally, special ceremonies such as the '''Seasonal Festivals''' (旬祭), the '''Kashiko-dokoro Ritual of Kanname-sai''' in October, and the '''Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura Ritual''' in December are held annually{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == Overview == The Three Palace Sanctuaries face south, with Kashiko-dokoro in the center, Kōreiden to the west, and Shinden to the east {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Kashiko-dokoro is considered the most noble building among the three sanctuaries, and its floor is designed to be about one shaku (approximately 30.3 cm) higher than the other two sanctuaries {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. Each sanctuary is divided into the '''outer sanctuary''' (a room where only [[imperial family]] members are allowed to enter), the '''inner sanctuary''' (a room where only the Emperor and Empress are allowed to enter), and the '''innermost sanctuary''' (a room where [[Amaterasu Omikami]] is enshrined). In the inner sanctuary of Kashiko-dokoro, there are two {{ill|eternal lamps|ja|常夜灯|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, it was also called Naishidokoro ({{ill|内侍所|ja|内侍所|12=draft}}) {{Efn2|神鏡は天皇の御座所に祀られていたが、その後に別殿の[[温明殿]]に移され、内侍所とも称されるようになった{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}。例えば『{{ill|更級日記|ja|更級日記|12=draft}}』に内侍所とある{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=22}}。『{{ill|源氏物語|ja|源氏物語|12=draft}}』の「{{ill|真木柱|ja|真木柱|12=draft}}」にも内侍所とあり、11月には祭祀が夥しく行われていたことが反映されている{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}。なお、別殿に移された時期について、『{{ill|江家次第|ja|江家次第|12=draft}}』や『{{ill|禁秘抄|ja|禁秘抄|12=draft}}』は垂仁天皇の代とし、『{{ill|撰集抄|ja|撰集抄|12=draft}}』は{{ill|宇多天皇|ja|宇多天皇|12=draft}}の代としている{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|pp=59-60}}。}}. Various characters were used for "Kashiko-dokoro" such as "恐所," "畏所," "威所," "尊所," and "貴所" {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Every morning, rituals are conducted by the {{ill|naishoten|ja|内掌典|12=draft}} and [[Shinto ritual|shinji]] are performed by an envoy dispatched by the Emperor without fail {{Sfnp|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Various rituals are also conducted, such as the Emperor's worship on the first day of the month, the [[Kanname-sai|Kanname-sai Kashiko-dokoro ceremony]] (a grand festival) in October, and the [[Kashiko-dokoro Misogi|Kashiko-dokoro Misogi ceremony]] (a minor festival) in December {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == History == The sacred mirror bestowed by [[Amaterasu Omikami]] has been enshrined within the Imperial Palace by successive generations of emperors according to the "Same Bed, Same Hall" divine decree {{Sfnp|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], during the reign of the tenth Emperor [[Suinin|Sujin]], the sacred mirror was moved outside the palace for enshrinement due to "living together being uneasy" {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|この神鏡は{{ill|垂仁天皇|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft}}の代に{{ill|伊勢国|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}の{{ill|五十鈴川|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft}}上流に移され、後に{{ill|伊勢神宮|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}となる{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}。}}. According to the {{ill|Kogo Shuui|ja|古語拾遺|12=draft}}, at that time, a duplicate of the sacred mirror was newly cast for enshrinement in the palace {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=54}}. From the mid-Heian period onward, with the burning of the {{ill|Dairi|ja|内裏|12=draft}} and other reasons, temporary palaces were established, and with them, Kashiko-dokoro was also relocated to appropriate buildings {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. In later years, Kashiko-dokoro was moved to the {{ill|Shunkoden|ja|春興殿|12=draft}}, and according to the descriptions in the {{ill|Meimosho|ja|名目抄|12=draft}} by {{ill|Toin Sanehiro|ja|洞院実熙|12=draft}}, by the early Muromachi period, Shunkoden had become Kashiko-dokoro {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. From the Jōkyō and Genroku periods of the Edo era, commoners were allowed to visit Naishidokoro on [[Setsubun]], and it became bustling with many visitors {{Sfnp|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In the early Meiji period, it was relocated to Tokyo, and according to a decree in September of the 4th year of Meiji (1871), shrines including Kashiko-dokoro were constructed {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. == History == The sacred mirror, bestowed by '''Amaterasu''', was enshrined in the Imperial Palace per the divine command ('''Same Chamber, Same Hall''') and has been worshiped by successive emperors{{Sfn|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the '''Nihon Shoki''', during the reign of Emperor Sujin (崇神天皇), the sacred mirror was relocated outside the palace for more secure worship{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|This mirror was eventually moved to the '''Ise Shrine''' during the reign of Emperor Suinin (垂仁天皇){{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}.}}. The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' experienced relocations due to the destruction of the '''Dairi''' (Imperial Residence) in the Heian period and the establishment of '''temporary residences''' ('''Satodairi'''){{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. By the early Muromachi period, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' had moved to the '''Shunki-den''', as noted in '''Naimokushō''' by '''Dōin Sanehiro'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. During the '''Ōnin War''', the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was temporarily relocated to the '''Muromachi Palace''', where it regained its position as the priority site for Imperial rituals{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52-53}}. By the Edo period, public access to the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was permitted during the '''Setsubun''' festival, attracting numerous visitors{{Sfn|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In 1871, during the Meiji era, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was officially reconstructed as part of the newly relocated '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. ==See also== * {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro Riding Car|ja|賢所乗御車|12=draft}} * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kyūchū Sanden|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura|ja|賢所御神楽|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kinri Koban|ja|禁裏小番|12=draft}} == Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}} ===References=== {{Reflist|3}} == References == ; Books * {{Cite Various kanji have been used for "Kashiko-dokoro," including '''恐所''', '''畏所''', '''威所''', '''尊所''', and '''貴所'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Daily offerings ('''Nikkū''') are conducted in the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' by the '''Naishoten''', and Imperial rituals ('''Myōchō Goyōhai''') are performed daily without interruption{{Sfn|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Additionally, special ceremonies such as the '''Seasonal Festivals''' (旬祭), the '''Kashiko-dokoro Ritual of Kanname-sai''' in October, and the '''Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura Ritual''' in December are held annually{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == Overview == The Three Palace Sanctuaries face south, with Kashiko-dokoro in the center, Kōreiden to the west, and Shinden to the east {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Kashiko-dokoro is considered the most noble building among the three sanctuaries, and its floor is designed to be about one shaku (approximately 30.3 cm) higher than the other two sanctuaries {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. Each sanctuary is divided into the '''outer sanctuary''' (a room where only [[imperial family]] members are allowed to enter), the '''inner sanctuary''' (a room where only the Emperor and Empress are allowed to enter), and the '''innermost sanctuary''' (a room where [[Amaterasu Omikami]] is enshrined). In the inner sanctuary of Kashiko-dokoro, there are two {{ill|eternal lamps|ja|常夜灯|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, it was also called Naishidokoro ({{ill|内侍所|ja|内侍所|12=draft}}) {{Efn2|神鏡は天皇の御座所に祀られていたが、その後に別殿の[[温明殿]]に移され、内侍所とも称されるようになった{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}。例えば『{{ill|更級日記|ja|更級日記|12=draft}}』に内侍所とある{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=22}}。『{{ill|源氏物語|ja|源氏物語|12=draft}}』の「{{ill|真木柱|ja|真木柱|12=draft}}」にも内侍所とあり、11月には祭祀が夥しく行われていたことが反映されている{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}。なお、別殿に移された時期について、『{{ill|江家次第|ja|江家次第|12=draft}}』や『{{ill|禁秘抄|ja|禁秘抄|12=draft}}』は垂仁天皇の代とし、『{{ill|撰集抄|ja|撰集抄|12=draft}}』は{{ill|宇多天皇|ja|宇多天皇|12=draft}}の代としている{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|pp=59-60}}。}}. Various characters were used for "Kashiko-dokoro" such as "恐所," "畏所," "威所," "尊所," and "貴所" {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Every morning, rituals are conducted by the {{ill|naishoten|ja|内掌典|12=draft}} and [[Shinto ritual|shinji]] are performed by an envoy dispatched by the Emperor without fail {{Sfnp|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Various rituals are also conducted, such as the Emperor's worship on the first day of the month, the [[Kanname-sai|Kanname-sai Kashiko-dokoro ceremony]] (a grand festival) in October, and the [[Kashiko-dokoro Misogi|Kashiko-dokoro Misogi ceremony]] (a minor festival) in December {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == History == The sacred mirror bestowed by [[Amaterasu Omikami]] has been enshrined within the Imperial Palace by successive generations of emperors according to the "Same Bed, Same Hall" divine decree {{Sfnp|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], during the reign of the tenth Emperor [[Suinin|Sujin]], the sacred mirror was moved outside the palace for enshrinement due to "living together being uneasy" {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|この神鏡は{{ill|垂仁天皇|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft}}の代に{{ill|伊勢国|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}の{{ill|五十鈴川|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft}}上流に移され、後に{{ill|伊勢神宮|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}となる{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}。}}. According to the {{ill|Kogo Shuui|ja|古語拾遺|12=draft}}, at that time, a duplicate of the sacred mirror was newly cast for enshrinement in the palace {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=54}}. From the mid-Heian period onward, with the burning of the {{ill|Dairi|ja|内裏|12=draft}} and other reasons, temporary palaces were established, and with them, Kashiko-dokoro was also relocated to appropriate buildings {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. In later years, Kashiko-dokoro was moved to the {{ill|Shunkoden|ja|春興殿|12=draft}}, and according to the descriptions in the {{ill|Meimosho|ja|名目抄|12=draft}} by {{ill|Toin Sanehiro|ja|洞院実熙|12=draft}}, by the early Muromachi period, Shunkoden had become Kashiko-dokoro {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. From the Jōkyō and Genroku periods of the Edo era, commoners were allowed to visit Naishidokoro on [[Setsubun]], and it became bustling with many visitors {{Sfnp|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In the early Meiji period, it was relocated to Tokyo, and according to a decree in September of the 4th year of Meiji (1871), shrines including Kashiko-dokoro were constructed {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. == History == The sacred mirror, bestowed by '''Amaterasu''', was enshrined in the Imperial Palace per the divine command ('''Same Chamber, Same Hall''') and has been worshiped by successive emperors{{Sfn|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the '''Nihon Shoki''', during the reign of Emperor Sujin (崇神天皇), the sacred mirror was relocated outside the palace for more secure worship{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|This mirror was eventually moved to the '''Ise Shrine''' during the reign of Emperor Suinin (垂仁天皇){{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}.}}. The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' experienced relocations due to the destruction of the '''Dairi''' (Imperial Residence) in the Heian period and the establishment of '''temporary residences''' ('''Satodairi'''){{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. By the early Muromachi period, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' had moved to the '''Shunki-den''', as noted in '''Naimokushō''' by '''Dōin Sanehiro'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. During the '''Ōnin War''', the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was temporarily relocated to the '''Muromachi Palace''', where it regained its position as the priority site for Imperial rituals{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52-53}}. By the Edo period, public access to the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was permitted during the '''Setsubun''' festival, attracting numerous visitors{{Sfn|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In 1871, during the Meiji era, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was officially reconstructed as part of the newly relocated '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. ==See also== * {{draft}}
'''Kashiko-dokoro''' (賢所, also read as Kensho{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is the location within the '''Imperial Palace''' (宮中) of Japan where the '''{{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}''', one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan|'''Three Sacred Treasures''']] (三種の神器) and the sacred mirror of the goddess '''[[Amaterasu]]''', is enshrined. Currently, it is one of the '''Kyūchū Sanden''' (宮中三殿) in the '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Before the modern era, it was also called '''Naishidokoro''' (内侍所){{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. '''Kashiko-dokoro''' (かしこどころ、けんしょ{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) is a place in the [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Emperor]]'s [[imperial court]] where the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}), one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan]] and the spirit of [[Amaterasu Omikami]], is enshrined. In the current [[Imperial Palace]] it is one of the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. [[File:Three Palace Sanctuaries.jpg|290px]] == Overview == {{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}} == Overview == The {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft|12=draft}} '''(Kyūchū Sanden)''' face south and consist of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' at the center, the '''Kōreiden''' to the west, and the '''Shinden''' to the east{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Among these three sanctuaries, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is regarded as the most sacred, and its floor is raised approximately one shaku (about 30 centimeters) higher than the others{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. The structure is divided into three sections: * '''Outer Chamber''' (外陣): Accessible only to members of the Imperial family. * '''Inner Chamber''' (内陣): Accessible only to the Emperor and Empress. * '''Innermost Chamber''' (内々陣): The space where the sacred mirror and '''Amaterasu''' are enshrined. The '''Inner Chamber''' of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' features two '''eternal lamps''' (常夜灯){{Sfn|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was also known as '''Naishidokoro''', referencing the sacred mirror's earlier placement in the '''Onmeiden''' before its relocation to a separate hall{{Efn2|The transition of the sacred mirror from the Emperor’s chamber to the Onmeiden, and later its designation as Naishidokoro, is detailed in several sources, such as the {{ill|Sarashina Nikki|ja|更級日記|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|The Tale of Genji|ja|源氏物語|12=draft|12=draft}}'s "Makibashira" chapter{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}.}}. Various kanji have been used for "Kashiko-dokoro," including '''恐所''', '''畏所''', '''威所''', '''尊所''', and '''貴所'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Daily offerings ('''Nikkū''') are conducted in the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' by the '''Naishoten''', and Imperial rituals ('''Myōchō Goyōhai''') are performed daily without interruption{{Sfn|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Additionally, special ceremonies such as the '''Seasonal Festivals''' (旬祭), the '''Kashiko-dokoro Ritual of Kanname-sai''' in October, and the '''Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura Ritual''' in December are held annually{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == Overview == The Three Palace Sanctuaries face south, with Kashiko-dokoro in the center, Kōreiden to the west, and Shinden to the east {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Kashiko-dokoro is considered the most noble building among the three sanctuaries, and its floor is designed to be about one shaku (approximately 30.3 cm) higher than the other two sanctuaries {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. Each sanctuary is divided into the '''outer sanctuary''' (a room where only [[imperial family]] members are allowed to enter), the '''inner sanctuary''' (a room where only the Emperor and Empress are allowed to enter), and the '''innermost sanctuary''' (a room where [[Amaterasu Omikami]] is enshrined). In the inner sanctuary of Kashiko-dokoro, there are two {{ill|eternal lamps|ja|常夜灯|12=draft|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, it was also called Naishidokoro ({{ill|内侍所|ja|内侍所|12=draft|12=draft}}) {{Efn2|神鏡は天皇の御座所に祀られていたが、その後に別殿の[[温明殿]]に移され、内侍所とも称されるようになった{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}。例えば『{{ill|更級日記|ja|更級日記|12=draft|12=draft}}』に内侍所とある{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=22}}。『{{ill|源氏物語|ja|源氏物語|12=draft|12=draft}}』の「{{ill|真木柱|ja|真木柱|12=draft|12=draft}}」にも内侍所とあり、11月には祭祀が夥しく行われていたことが反映されている{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}。なお、別殿に移された時期について、『{{ill|江家次第|ja|江家次第|12=draft|12=draft}}』や『{{ill|禁秘抄|ja|禁秘抄|12=draft|12=draft}}』は垂仁天皇の代とし、『{{ill|撰集抄|ja|撰集抄|12=draft|12=draft}}』は{{ill|宇多天皇|ja|宇多天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}の代としている{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|pp=59-60}}。}}. Various characters were used for "Kashiko-dokoro" such as "恐所," "畏所," "威所," "尊所," and "貴所" {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Every morning, rituals are conducted by the {{ill|naishoten|ja|内掌典|12=draft|12=draft}} and [[Shinto ritual|shinji]] are performed by an envoy dispatched by the Emperor without fail {{Sfnp|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Various rituals are also conducted, such as the Emperor's worship on the first day of the month, the [[Kanname-sai|Kanname-sai Kashiko-dokoro ceremony]] (a grand festival) in October, and the [[Kashiko-dokoro Misogi|Kashiko-dokoro Misogi ceremony]] (a minor festival) in December {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == History == The sacred mirror bestowed by [[Amaterasu Omikami]] has been enshrined within the Imperial Palace by successive generations of emperors according to the "Same Bed, Same Hall" divine decree {{Sfnp|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], during the reign of the tenth Emperor [[Suinin|Sujin]], the sacred mirror was moved outside the palace for enshrinement due to "living together being uneasy" {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|この神鏡は{{ill|垂仁天皇|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}の代に{{ill|伊勢国|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft}}の{{ill|五十鈴川|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft|12=draft}}上流に移され、後に{{ill|伊勢神宮|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}となる{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}。}}. According to the {{ill|Kogo Shuui|ja|古語拾遺|12=draft|12=draft}}, at that time, a duplicate of the sacred mirror was newly cast for enshrinement in the palace {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=54}}. From the mid-Heian period onward, with the burning of the {{ill|Dairi|ja|内裏|12=draft|12=draft}} and other reasons, temporary palaces were established, and with them, Kashiko-dokoro was also relocated to appropriate buildings {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. In later years, Kashiko-dokoro was moved to the {{ill|Shunkoden|ja|春興殿|12=draft|12=draft}}, and according to the descriptions in the {{ill|Meimosho|ja|名目抄|12=draft|12=draft}} by {{ill|Toin Sanehiro|ja|洞院実熙|12=draft|12=draft}}, by the early Muromachi period, Shunkoden had become Kashiko-dokoro {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. From the Jōkyō and Genroku periods of the Edo era, commoners were allowed to visit Naishidokoro on [[Setsubun]], and it became bustling with many visitors {{Sfnp|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In the early Meiji period, it was relocated to Tokyo, and according to a decree in September of the 4th year of Meiji (1871), shrines including Kashiko-dokoro were constructed {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. == History == The sacred mirror, bestowed by '''Amaterasu''', was enshrined in the Imperial Palace per the divine command ('''Same Chamber, Same Hall''') and has been worshiped by successive emperors{{Sfn|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the '''Nihon Shoki''', during the reign of Emperor Sujin (崇神天皇), the sacred mirror was relocated outside the palace for more secure worship{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|This mirror was eventually moved to the '''Ise Shrine''' during the reign of Emperor Suinin (垂仁天皇){{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}.}}. The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' experienced relocations due to the destruction of the '''Dairi''' (Imperial Residence) in the Heian period and the establishment of '''temporary residences''' ('''Satodairi'''){{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. By the early Muromachi period, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' had moved to the '''Shunki-den''', as noted in '''Naimokushō''' by '''Dōin Sanehiro'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. During the '''Ōnin War''', the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was temporarily relocated to the '''Muromachi Palace''', where it regained its position as the priority site for Imperial rituals{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52-53}}. By the Edo period, public access to the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was permitted during the '''Setsubun''' festival, attracting numerous visitors{{Sfn|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In 1871, during the Meiji era, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was officially reconstructed as part of the newly relocated '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. ==See also== * revision.text:
{{draft}}
'''Kashiko-dokoro''' (賢所, also read as Kensho{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is the location within the '''Imperial Palace''' (宮中) of Japan where the '''{{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}''', one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan|'''Three Sacred Treasures''']] (三種の神器) and the sacred mirror of the goddess '''[[Amaterasu]]''', is enshrined. Currently, it is one of the '''Kyūchū Sanden''' (宮中三殿) in the '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Before the modern era, it was also called '''Naishidokoro''' (内侍所){{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. '''Kashiko-dokoro''' (かしこどころ、けんしょ{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) is a place in the [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Emperor]]'s [[imperial court]] where the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}), one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan]] and the spirit of [[Amaterasu Omikami]], is enshrined. In the current [[Imperial Palace]] it is one of the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. [[File:Three Palace Sanctuaries.jpg|290px]] == Overview == {{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}} == Overview == The {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} '''(Kyūchū Sanden)''' face south and consist of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' at the center, the '''Kōreiden''' to the west, and the '''Shinden''' to the east{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Among these three sanctuaries, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is regarded as the most sacred, and its floor is raised approximately one shaku (about 30 centimeters) higher than the others{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. The structure is divided into three sections: * '''Outer Chamber''' (外陣): Accessible only to members of the Imperial family. * '''Inner Chamber''' (内陣): Accessible only to the Emperor and Empress. * '''Innermost Chamber''' (内々陣): The space where the sacred mirror and '''Amaterasu''' are enshrined. The '''Inner Chamber''' of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' features two '''eternal lamps''' (常夜灯){{Sfn|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was also known as '''Naishidokoro''', referencing the sacred mirror's earlier placement in the '''Onmeiden''' before its relocation to a separate hall{{Efn2|The transition of the sacred mirror from the Emperor’s chamber to the Onmeiden, and later its designation as Naishidokoro, is detailed in several sources, such as the {{ill|Sarashina Nikki|ja|更級日記|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|The Tale of Genji|ja|源氏物語|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}'s "Makibashira" chapter{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}.}}. Various kanji have been used for "Kashiko-dokoro," including '''恐所''', '''畏所''', '''威所''', '''尊所''', and '''貴所'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Daily offerings ('''Nikkū''') are conducted in the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' by the '''Naishoten''', and Imperial rituals ('''Myōchō Goyōhai''') are performed daily without interruption{{Sfn|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Additionally, special ceremonies such as the '''Seasonal Festivals''' (旬祭), the '''Kashiko-dokoro Ritual of Kanname-sai''' in October, and the '''Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura Ritual''' in December are held annually{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == Overview == The Three Palace Sanctuaries face south, with Kashiko-dokoro in the center, Kōreiden to the west, and Shinden to the east {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Kashiko-dokoro is considered the most noble building among the three sanctuaries, and its floor is designed to be about one shaku (approximately 30.3 cm) higher than the other two sanctuaries {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. Each sanctuary is divided into the '''outer sanctuary''' (a room where only [[imperial family]] members are allowed to enter), the '''inner sanctuary''' (a room where only the Emperor and Empress are allowed to enter), and the '''innermost sanctuary''' (a room where [[Amaterasu Omikami]] is enshrined). In the inner sanctuary of Kashiko-dokoro, there are two {{ill|eternal lamps|ja|常夜灯|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, it was also called Naishidokoro ({{ill|内侍所|ja|内侍所|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) {{Efn2|神鏡は天皇の御座所に祀られていたが、その後に別殿の[[温明殿]]に移され、内侍所とも称されるようになった{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}。例えば『{{ill|更級日記|ja|更級日記|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』に内侍所とある{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=22}}。『{{ill|源氏物語|ja|源氏物語|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』の「{{ill|真木柱|ja|真木柱|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}」にも内侍所とあり、11月には祭祀が夥しく行われていたことが反映されている{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}。なお、別殿に移された時期について、『{{ill|江家次第|ja|江家次第|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』や『{{ill|禁秘抄|ja|禁秘抄|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』は垂仁天皇の代とし、『{{ill|撰集抄|ja|撰集抄|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』は{{ill|宇多天皇|ja|宇多天皇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}の代としている{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|pp=59-60}}。}}. Various characters were used for "Kashiko-dokoro" such as "恐所," "畏所," "威所," "尊所," and "貴所" {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Every morning, rituals are conducted by the {{ill|naishoten|ja|内掌典|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and [[Shinto ritual|shinji]] are performed by an envoy dispatched by the Emperor without fail {{Sfnp|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Various rituals are also conducted, such as the Emperor's worship on the first day of the month, the [[Kanname-sai|Kanname-sai Kashiko-dokoro ceremony]] (a grand festival) in October, and the [[Kashiko-dokoro Misogi|Kashiko-dokoro Misogi ceremony]] (a minor festival) in December {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == History == The sacred mirror bestowed by [[Amaterasu Omikami]] has been enshrined within the Imperial Palace by successive generations of emperors according to the "Same Bed, Same Hall" divine decree {{Sfnp|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], during the reign of the tenth Emperor [[Suinin|Sujin]], the sacred mirror was moved outside the palace for enshrinement due to "living together being uneasy" {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|この神鏡は{{ill|垂仁天皇|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}の代に{{ill|伊勢国|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}の{{ill|五十鈴川|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}上流に移され、後に{{ill|伊勢神宮|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}となる{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}。}}. According to the {{ill|Kogo Shuui|ja|古語拾遺|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, at that time, a duplicate of the sacred mirror was newly cast for enshrinement in the palace {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=54}}. From the mid-Heian period onward, with the burning of the {{ill|Dairi|ja|内裏|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and other reasons, temporary palaces were established, and with them, Kashiko-dokoro was also relocated to appropriate buildings {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. In later years, Kashiko-dokoro was moved to the {{ill|Shunkoden|ja|春興殿|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and according to the descriptions in the {{ill|Meimosho|ja|名目抄|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} by {{ill|Toin Sanehiro|ja|洞院実熙|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, by the early Muromachi period, Shunkoden had become Kashiko-dokoro {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. From the Jōkyō and Genroku periods of the Edo era, commoners were allowed to visit Naishidokoro on [[Setsubun]], and it became bustling with many visitors {{Sfnp|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In the early Meiji period, it was relocated to Tokyo, and according to a decree in September of the 4th year of Meiji (1871), shrines including Kashiko-dokoro were constructed {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. == History == The sacred mirror, bestowed by '''Amaterasu''', was enshrined in the Imperial Palace per the divine command ('''Same Chamber, Same Hall''') and has been worshiped by successive emperors{{Sfn|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the '''Nihon Shoki''', during the reign of Emperor Sujin (崇神天皇), the sacred mirror was relocated outside the palace for more secure worship{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|This mirror was eventually moved to the '''Ise Shrine''' during the reign of Emperor Suinin (垂仁天皇){{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}.}}. The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' experienced relocations due to the destruction of the '''Dairi''' (Imperial Residence) in the Heian period and the establishment of '''temporary residences''' ('''Satodairi'''){{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. By the early Muromachi period, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' had moved to the '''Shunki-den''', as noted in '''Naimokushō''' by '''Dōin Sanehiro'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. During the '''Ōnin War''', the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was temporarily relocated to the '''Muromachi Palace''', where it regained its position as the priority site for Imperial rituals{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52-53}}. By the Edo period, public access to the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was permitted during the '''Setsubun''' festival, attracting numerous visitors{{Sfn|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In 1871, during the Meiji era, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was officially reconstructed as part of the newly relocated '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. ==See also== * {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro Riding Car|ja|賢所乗御車|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kyūchū Sanden|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura|ja|賢所御神楽|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kinri Koban|ja|禁裏小番|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} == {{draft}}
'''Kashiko-dokoro''' (賢所, also read as Kensho{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is the location within the '''Imperial Palace''' (宮中) of Japan where the '''{{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}''', one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan|'''Three Sacred Treasures''']] (三種の神器) and the sacred mirror of the goddess '''[[Amaterasu]]''', is enshrined. Currently, it is one of the '''Kyūchū Sanden''' (宮中三殿) in the '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Before the modern era, it was also called '''Naishidokoro''' (内侍所){{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. '''Kashiko-dokoro''' (かしこどころ、けんしょ{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) is a place in the [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Emperor]]'s [[imperial court]] where the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}), one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan]] and the spirit of [[Amaterasu Omikami]], is enshrined. In the current [[Imperial Palace]] it is one of the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. [[File:Three Palace Sanctuaries.jpg|290px]] == Overview == {{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}} == Overview == The {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} '''(Kyūchū Sanden)''' face south and consist of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' at the center, the '''Kōreiden''' to the west, and the '''Shinden''' to the east{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Among these three sanctuaries, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is regarded as the most sacred, and its floor is raised approximately one shaku (about 30 centimeters) higher than the others{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. The structure is divided into three sections: * '''Outer Chamber''' (外陣): Accessible only to members of the Imperial family. * '''Inner Chamber''' (内陣): Accessible only to the Emperor and Empress. * '''Innermost Chamber''' (内々陣): The space where the sacred mirror and '''Amaterasu''' are enshrined. The '''Inner Chamber''' of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' features two '''eternal lamps''' (常夜灯){{Sfn|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was also known as '''Naishidokoro''', referencing the sacred mirror's earlier placement in the '''Onmeiden''' before its relocation to a separate hall{{Efn2|The transition of the sacred mirror from the Emperor’s chamber to the Onmeiden, and later its designation as Naishidokoro, is detailed in several sources, such as the {{ill|Sarashina Nikki|ja|更級日記|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|The Tale of Genji|ja|源氏物語|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}'s "Makibashira" chapter{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}.}}. Various kanji have been used for "Kashiko-dokoro," including '''恐所''', '''畏所''', '''威所''', '''尊所''', and '''貴所'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Daily offerings ('''Nikkū''') are conducted in the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' by the '''Naishoten''', and Imperial rituals ('''Myōchō Goyōhai''') are performed daily without interruption{{Sfn|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Additionally, special ceremonies such as the '''Seasonal Festivals''' (旬祭), the '''Kashiko-dokoro Ritual of Kanname-sai''' in October, and the '''Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura Ritual''' in December are held annually{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == Overview == The Three Palace Sanctuaries face south, with Kashiko-dokoro in the center, Kōreiden to the west, and Shinden to the east {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Kashiko-dokoro is considered the most noble building among the three sanctuaries, and its floor is designed to be about one shaku (approximately 30.3 cm) higher than the other two sanctuaries {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. Each sanctuary is divided into the '''outer sanctuary''' (a room where only [[imperial family]] members are allowed to enter), the '''inner sanctuary''' (a room where only the Emperor and Empress are allowed to enter), and the '''innermost sanctuary''' (a room where [[Amaterasu Omikami]] is enshrined). In the inner sanctuary of Kashiko-dokoro, there are two {{ill|eternal lamps|ja|常夜灯|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, it was also called Naishidokoro ({{ill|内侍所|ja|内侍所|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) {{Efn2|神鏡は天皇の御座所に祀られていたが、その後に別殿の[[温明殿]]に移され、内侍所とも称されるようになった{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}。例えば『{{ill|更級日記|ja|更級日記|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』に内侍所とある{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=22}}。『{{ill|源氏物語|ja|源氏物語|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』の「{{ill|真木柱|ja|真木柱|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}」にも内侍所とあり、11月には祭祀が夥しく行われていたことが反映されている{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}。なお、別殿に移された時期について、『{{ill|江家次第|ja|江家次第|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』や『{{ill|禁秘抄|ja|禁秘抄|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』は垂仁天皇の代とし、『{{ill|撰集抄|ja|撰集抄|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』は{{ill|宇多天皇|ja|宇多天皇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}の代としている{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|pp=59-60}}。}}. Various characters were used for "Kashiko-dokoro" such as "恐所," "畏所," "威所," "尊所," and "貴所" {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Every morning, rituals are conducted by the {{ill|naishoten|ja|内掌典|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and [[Shinto ritual|shinji]] are performed by an envoy dispatched by the Emperor without fail {{Sfnp|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Various rituals are also conducted, such as the Emperor's worship on the first day of the month, the [[Kanname-sai|Kanname-sai Kashiko-dokoro ceremony]] (a grand festival) in October, and the [[Kashiko-dokoro Misogi|Kashiko-dokoro Misogi ceremony]] (a minor festival) in December {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == History == The sacred mirror bestowed by [[Amaterasu Omikami]] has been enshrined within the Imperial Palace by successive generations of emperors according to the "Same Bed, Same Hall" divine decree {{Sfnp|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], during the reign of the tenth Emperor [[Suinin|Sujin]], the sacred mirror was moved outside the palace for enshrinement due to "living together being uneasy" {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|この神鏡は{{ill|垂仁天皇|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}の代に{{ill|伊勢国|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}の{{ill|五十鈴川|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}上流に移され、後に{{ill|伊勢神宮|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}となる{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}。}}. According to the {{ill|Kogo Shuui|ja|古語拾遺|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, at that time, a duplicate of the sacred mirror was newly cast for enshrinement in the palace {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=54}}. From the mid-Heian period onward, with the burning of the {{ill|Dairi|ja|内裏|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and other reasons, temporary palaces were established, and with them, Kashiko-dokoro was also relocated to appropriate buildings {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. In later years, Kashiko-dokoro was moved to the {{ill|Shunkoden|ja|春興殿|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and according to the descriptions in the {{ill|Meimosho|ja|名目抄|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} by {{ill|Toin Sanehiro|ja|洞院実熙|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, by the early Muromachi period, Shunkoden had become Kashiko-dokoro {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. From the Jōkyō and Genroku periods of the Edo era, commoners were allowed to visit Naishidokoro on [[Setsubun]], and it became bustling with many visitors {{Sfnp|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In the early Meiji period, it was relocated to Tokyo, and according to a decree in September of the 4th year of Meiji (1871), shrines including Kashiko-dokoro were constructed {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. == History == The sacred mirror, bestowed by '''Amaterasu''', was enshrined in the Imperial Palace per the divine command ('''Same Chamber, Same Hall''') and has been worshiped by successive emperors{{Sfn|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the '''Nihon Shoki''', during the reign of Emperor Sujin (崇神天皇), the sacred mirror was relocated outside the palace for more secure worship{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|This mirror was eventually moved to the '''Ise Shrine''' during the reign of Emperor Suinin (垂仁天皇){{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}.}}. The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' experienced relocations due to the destruction of the '''Dairi''' (Imperial Residence) in the Heian period and the establishment of '''temporary residences''' ('''Satodairi'''){{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. By the early Muromachi period, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' had moved to the '''Shunki-den''', as noted in '''Naimokushō''' by '''Dōin Sanehiro'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. During the '''Ōnin War''', the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was temporarily relocated to the '''Muromachi Palace''', where it regained its position as the priority site for Imperial rituals{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52-53}}. By the Edo period, public access to the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was permitted during the '''Setsubun''' festival, attracting numerous visitors{{Sfn|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In 1871, during the Meiji era, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was officially reconstructed as part of the newly relocated '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. ==See also== * Every morning, rituals are conducted by the {{ill|naishoten|ja|内掌典|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and [[Shinto ritual|shinji]] are performed by an envoy dispatched by the Emperor without fail {{Sfnp|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Various rituals are also conducted, such as the Emperor's worship on the first day of the month, the [[Kanname-sai|Kanname-sai Kashiko-dokoro ceremony]] (a grand festival) in October, and the [[Kashiko-dokoro Misogi|Kashiko-dokoro Misogi ceremony]] (a minor festival) in December {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == History == The sacred mirror bestowed by [[Amaterasu Omikami]] has been enshrined within the Imperial Palace by successive generations of emperors according to the "Same Bed, Same Hall" divine decree {{Sfnp|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], during the reign of the tenth Emperor [[Suinin|Sujin]], the sacred mirror was moved outside the palace for enshrinement due to "living together being uneasy" {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|この神鏡は{{ill|垂仁天皇|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}の代に{{ill|伊勢国|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}の{{ill|五十鈴川|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}上流に移され、後に{{ill|伊勢神宮|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}となる{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}。}}. According to the {{ill|Kogo Shuui|ja|古語拾遺|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, at that time, a duplicate of the sacred mirror was newly cast for enshrinement in the palace {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=54}}. From the mid-Heian period onward, with the burning of the {{ill|Dairi|ja|内裏|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and other reasons, temporary palaces were established, and with them, Kashiko-dokoro was also relocated to appropriate buildings {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. In later years, Kashiko-dokoro was moved to the {{ill|Shunkoden|ja|春興殿|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and according to the descriptions in the {{ill|Meimosho|ja|名目抄|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} by {{ill|Toin Sanehiro|ja|洞院実熙|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, by the early Muromachi period, Shunkoden had become Kashiko-dokoro {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. From the Jōkyō and Genroku periods of the Edo era, commoners were allowed to visit Naishidokoro on [[Setsubun]], and it became bustling with many visitors {{Sfnp|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In the early Meiji period, it was relocated to Tokyo, and according to a decree in September of the 4th year of Meiji (1871), shrines including Kashiko-dokoro were constructed {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. == History == The sacred mirror, bestowed by '''Amaterasu''', was enshrined in the Imperial Palace per the divine command ('''Same Chamber, Same Hall''') and has been worshiped by successive emperors{{Sfn|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the '''Nihon Shoki''', during the reign of Emperor Sujin (崇神天皇), the sacred mirror was relocated outside the palace for more secure worship{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|This mirror was eventually moved to the '''Ise Shrine''' during the reign of Emperor Suinin (垂仁天皇){{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}.}}. The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' experienced relocations due to the destruction of the '''Dairi''' (Imperial Residence) in the Heian period and the establishment of '''temporary residences''' ('''Satodairi'''){{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. By the early Muromachi period, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' had moved to the '''Shunki-den''', as noted in '''Naimokushō''' by '''Dōin Sanehiro'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. During the '''Ōnin War''', the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was temporarily relocated to the '''Muromachi Palace''', where it regained its position as the priority site for Imperial rituals{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52-53}}. By the Edo period, public access to the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was permitted during the '''Setsubun''' festival, attracting numerous visitors{{Sfn|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In 1871, during the Meiji era, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was officially reconstructed as part of the newly relocated '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. ==See also== * {{draft}}
'''Kashiko-dokoro''' (賢所, also read as Kensho{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is the location within the '''Imperial Palace''' (宮中) of Japan where the '''{{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}''', one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan|'''Three Sacred Treasures''']] (三種の神器) and the sacred mirror of the goddess '''[[Amaterasu]]''', is enshrined. Currently, it is one of the '''Kyūchū Sanden''' (宮中三殿) in the '''Imperial Palace'''{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Before the modern era, it was also called '''Naishidokoro''' (内侍所){{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. '''Kashiko-dokoro''' (かしこどころ、けんしょ{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=2}}) is a place in the [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Emperor]]'s [[imperial court]] where the sacred mirror ({{ill|Yata no Kagami|ja|八咫鏡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}), one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures of Japan]] and the spirit of [[Amaterasu Omikami]], is enshrined. In the current [[Imperial Palace]] it is one of the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. [[File:Three Palace Sanctuaries.jpg|290px]] == Overview == {{User:Immanuelle/Japanese Imperial Rituals updated}} == Overview == The {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja|宮中三殿|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} '''(Kyūchū Sanden)''' face south and consist of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' at the center, the '''Kōreiden''' to the west, and the '''Shinden''' to the east{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Among these three sanctuaries, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' is regarded as the most sacred, and its floor is raised approximately one shaku (about 30 centimeters) higher than the others{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. The structure is divided into three sections: * '''Outer Chamber''' (外陣): Accessible only to members of the Imperial family. * '''Inner Chamber''' (内陣): Accessible only to the Emperor and Empress. * '''Innermost Chamber''' (内々陣): The space where the sacred mirror and '''Amaterasu''' are enshrined. The '''Inner Chamber''' of the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' features two '''eternal lamps''' (常夜灯){{Sfn|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was also known as '''Naishidokoro''', referencing the sacred mirror's earlier placement in the '''Onmeiden''' before its relocation to a separate hall{{Efn2|The transition of the sacred mirror from the Emperor’s chamber to the Onmeiden, and later its designation as Naishidokoro, is detailed in several sources, such as the {{ill|Sarashina Nikki|ja|更級日記|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|The Tale of Genji|ja|源氏物語|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}'s "Makibashira" chapter{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}.}}. Various kanji have been used for "Kashiko-dokoro," including '''恐所''', '''畏所''', '''威所''', '''尊所''', and '''貴所'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Daily offerings ('''Nikkū''') are conducted in the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' by the '''Naishoten''', and Imperial rituals ('''Myōchō Goyōhai''') are performed daily without interruption{{Sfn|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Additionally, special ceremonies such as the '''Seasonal Festivals''' (旬祭), the '''Kashiko-dokoro Ritual of Kanname-sai''' in October, and the '''Kashiko-dokoro Mikagura Ritual''' in December are held annually{{Sfn|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == Overview == The Three Palace Sanctuaries face south, with Kashiko-dokoro in the center, Kōreiden to the west, and Shinden to the east {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=45}}. Kashiko-dokoro is considered the most noble building among the three sanctuaries, and its floor is designed to be about one shaku (approximately 30.3 cm) higher than the other two sanctuaries {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}. Each sanctuary is divided into the '''outer sanctuary''' (a room where only [[imperial family]] members are allowed to enter), the '''inner sanctuary''' (a room where only the Emperor and Empress are allowed to enter), and the '''innermost sanctuary''' (a room where [[Amaterasu Omikami]] is enshrined). In the inner sanctuary of Kashiko-dokoro, there are two {{ill|eternal lamps|ja|常夜灯|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{Sfnp|皇室編集部|2017|pp=42-43}}. Historically, it was also called Naishidokoro ({{ill|内侍所|ja|内侍所|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) {{Efn2|神鏡は天皇の御座所に祀られていたが、その後に別殿の[[温明殿]]に移され、内侍所とも称されるようになった{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=59}}。例えば『{{ill|更級日記|ja|更級日記|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』に内侍所とある{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=22}}。『{{ill|源氏物語|ja|源氏物語|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』の「{{ill|真木柱|ja|真木柱|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}」にも内侍所とあり、11月には祭祀が夥しく行われていたことが反映されている{{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|p=46}}。なお、別殿に移された時期について、『{{ill|江家次第|ja|江家次第|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』や『{{ill|禁秘抄|ja|禁秘抄|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』は垂仁天皇の代とし、『{{ill|撰集抄|ja|撰集抄|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}』は{{ill|宇多天皇|ja|宇多天皇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}の代としている{{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|pp=59-60}}。}}. Various characters were used for "Kashiko-dokoro" such as "恐所," "畏所," "威所," "尊所," and "貴所" {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=1-2}}. Every morning, rituals are conducted by the {{ill|naishoten|ja|内掌典|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and [[Shinto ritual|shinji]] are performed by an envoy dispatched by the Emperor without fail {{Sfnp|松山能夫|1966|pp=121-123}}. Various rituals are also conducted, such as the Emperor's worship on the first day of the month, the [[Kanname-sai|Kanname-sai Kashiko-dokoro ceremony]] (a grand festival) in October, and the [[Kashiko-dokoro Misogi|Kashiko-dokoro Misogi ceremony]] (a minor festival) in December {{Sfnp|中澤伸弘|2010|pp=64-92}}. == History == The sacred mirror bestowed by [[Amaterasu Omikami]] has been enshrined within the Imperial Palace by successive generations of emperors according to the "Same Bed, Same Hall" divine decree {{Sfnp|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], during the reign of the tenth Emperor [[Suinin|Sujin]], the sacred mirror was moved outside the palace for enshrinement due to "living together being uneasy" {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|この神鏡は{{ill|垂仁天皇|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}の代に{{ill|伊勢国|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}の{{ill|五十鈴川|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}上流に移され、後に{{ill|伊勢神宮|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}となる{{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}。}}. According to the {{ill|Kogo Shuui|ja|古語拾遺|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, at that time, a duplicate of the sacred mirror was newly cast for enshrinement in the palace {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=54}}. From the mid-Heian period onward, with the burning of the {{ill|Dairi|ja|内裏|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and other reasons, temporary palaces were established, and with them, Kashiko-dokoro was also relocated to appropriate buildings {{Sfnp|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. In later years, Kashiko-dokoro was moved to the {{ill|Shunkoden|ja|春興殿|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and according to the descriptions in the {{ill|Meimosho|ja|名目抄|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} by {{ill|Toin Sanehiro|ja|洞院実熙|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, by the early Muromachi period, Shunkoden had become Kashiko-dokoro {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. From the Jōkyō and Genroku periods of the Edo era, commoners were allowed to visit Naishidokoro on [[Setsubun]], and it became bustling with many visitors {{Sfnp|藤田覚|2018|pp=207-208}}. In the early Meiji period, it was relocated to Tokyo, and according to a decree in September of the 4th year of Meiji (1871), shrines including Kashiko-dokoro were constructed {{Sfnp|皇典講究所|1908|pp=13-15}}. == History == The sacred mirror, bestowed by '''Amaterasu''', was enshrined in the Imperial Palace per the divine command ('''Same Chamber, Same Hall''') and has been worshiped by successive emperors{{Sfn|八束清貫|1957|p=4}}. According to the '''Nihon Shoki''', during the reign of Emperor Sujin (崇神天皇), the sacred mirror was relocated outside the palace for more secure worship{{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=53}}{{Efn2|This mirror was eventually moved to the '''Ise Shrine''' during the reign of Emperor Suinin (垂仁天皇){{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=4}}.}}. The '''Kashiko-dokoro''' experienced relocations due to the destruction of the '''Dairi''' (Imperial Residence) in the Heian period and the establishment of '''temporary residences''' ('''Satodairi'''){{Sfn|橋本富太郎|2005|p=65}}. By the early Muromachi period, the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' had moved to the '''Shunki-den''', as noted in '''Naimokushō''' by '''Dōin Sanehiro'''{{Sfn|皇典講究所|1908|p=12}}. During the '''Ōnin War''', the '''Kashiko-dokoro''' was temporarily relocated to the '''Muromachi Palace''', where it regained its position as the priority site for Imperial rituals{{Sfn|中本真人|2021|p=52-53}}.
